burnoutContent.name: Maslach Burnout Inventory Dimensions
burnoutContent.description: The three core dimensions of burnout: Emotional Exhaustion, Depersonalization (cynicism), and Reduced Personal Accomplishment.
examples: Feeling utterly drained before the workday even begins (Exhaustion).
Treating clients or colleagues as impersonal objects rather than people (Depersonalization).
reframingQuestions: Which of these three dimensions is affecting me the most right now?
Am I viewing my colleagues cynically, or am I just deeply exhausted?

burnoutContent.name: Recovery Strategies
burnoutContent.description: Active and passive techniques used to replenish physical and psychological resources depleted by chronic stress.
examples: Taking actual, unplugged vacations without checking work emails.
Engaging in restorative hobbies like gardening, painting, or yoga.
reframingQuestions: What activities actually make me feel recharged rather than just distracted?
How can I proactively schedule recovery time into my weekly routine?

burnoutContent.name: Work-Life Balance Techniques
burnoutContent.description: Methods for creating a sustainable equilibrium between professional demands and personal life.
examples: Leaving work equipment at the office to prevent working from home.
Designating specific 'no-screen' hours during the evening to focus on family.
reframingQuestions: What does a healthy balance between my work and personal life actually look like?
Are my current daily habits supporting my personal life, or eroding it?

burnoutContent.name: Boundary Setting
burnoutContent.description: Communicating and enforcing clear limits regarding time, energy, and responsibilities to protect well-being.
examples: Politely declining to take on a new project when already at capacity.
Setting an auto-responder stating you do not check emails after 6:00 PM.
reframingQuestions: What is a reasonable limit I need to set to protect my mental health?
How can I communicate my boundaries clearly but respectfully to my team?
